items.front: What two things must you do when you receive a condition report affecting safe use of a landing area?
items.back: Copy it verbatim and record the name of the person submitting it.

items.front: What triggers the requirement to confirm a landing-area condition report?
items.back: When the report is obtained from someone other than authorized airport or FAA personnel.

items.front: What happens when an updated RwyCC arrives but the ATIS broadcast hasn't been updated yet?
items.back: Verbally issue the updated codes to all aircraft until the ATIS can be updated.

items.front: Who can legally close a runway, and what is the controller's fallback if that authority is unreachable?
items.back: Only airport management or military operations can close a runway. The controller issues a NOTAM publicizing the unsafe condition and informs management as soon as practicable. The controller does not close the runway.

items.front: What are the three escalating steps in the 3-3-2 closed-runway procedure?
items.back: 1. Inform pilot the runway is closed/unsafe. 2. If pilot persists, quote the NOTAM and state clearance cannot be issued. 3. If pilot still insists and no adverse traffic impact, state operation will be at pilot's own risk (no clearance issued).

items.front: Does the phrase 'LANDING WILL BE AT YOUR OWN RISK' constitute a clearance?
items.back: No. It is a statement of risk acceptance, never a clearance. The controller never issues a takeoff or landing clearance for a closed or unsafe runway.

items.front: Under what narrow circumstances may the ILS associated with a closed runway be used for approaches?
items.back: 1) When the side-step exception applies (para 4-8-7). 2) When not using that ILS would adversely affect airport operational efficiency.

items.front: What is the timing standard for issuing airport condition information?
items.back: In time for it to be useful to the pilot for safe operation.

items.front: Why does 3-3-3 require stating whether volcanic ash is wet or dry?
items.back: Wet ash degrades braking action. Dry ash may necessitate minimum use of reverse thrust to avoid engine ingestion.

items.front: List the eight categories of airport condition information in 3-3-3.
items.back: 1) Construction on/near movement area. 2) Rough movement area portions. 3) Braking conditions (ice, snow, slush, water). 4) Snowdrifts/piles and plowed extent. 5) Parked aircraft on movement area. 6) Irregular airport lighting. 7) Volcanic ash (wet/dry if known). 8) Other pertinent conditions.

items.front: What are the six approved braking-action terms in order?
items.back: Good, good to medium, medium, medium to poor, poor, nil.

items.front: What does the term 'nil' mean in braking action?
items.back: Bad or no braking action.

items.front: How does RCR distribution differ between USAF/ANG aircraft and civil aircraft?
items.back: USAF/ANG: issued automatically with runway surface condition. Civil: issued only upon pilot request.

items.front: Why should braking-action location be described with runway portions rather than landmarks?
items.back: Landmarks extraneous to the runway (fire station, taxiway names) are hard for a pilot to identify during low visibility, at night, or while busy landing.

items.front: What two independent conditions trigger putting Braking Action Advisories on the ATIS?
items.back: 1) Pilot braking reports include medium, poor, or nil. 2) Weather conditions are conducive to deteriorating or rapidly changing runway conditions. Either alone is sufficient.

items.front: What four actions must be taken during Braking Action Advisories?
items.back: 1) Issue latest braking report to each arriving and departing aircraft (prefer same weight class for super/heavy). 2) If no report exists for the runway, issue NO BRAKING ACTION REPORTS advisory. 3) Advise the Airport Operator of degraded braking reports (good to medium through nil). 4) Solicit PIREPs of runway braking action.

items.front: Which braking action report levels require notifying the Airport Operator under 3-3-5b(3)?
items.back: Good to medium, medium, medium to poor, poor, and nil. Note: 'good' is NOT included — only degraded reports trigger operator notification.

items.front: What are the three independent triggers for raising an aircraft arresting system?
items.back: 1) Pilot request (emergency: 'BARRIER BARRIER BARRIER' or 'CABLE CABLE CABLE'). 2) Military authority request. 3) Military jet landing with known/suspected conditions (drag chute/hydraulic/electrical failure) indicating the system may be needed.

items.front: When a barrier or cable request is made, which end of the runway does it refer to by default?
items.back: The departure end. The approach end must be specifically requested if needed.

items.front: What is the difference in crash alarm requirements between an emergency barrier engagement and a preplanned practice engagement?
items.back: Emergency engagement: initiate crash alarm immediately. Preplanned practice: crash alarm need not be activated if all required notifications were made beforehand per local military procedures.

items.front: When may an arresting system remain in the raised position continuously?
items.back: When requested by military authority due to freezing weather or activating mechanism malfunction, provided aircraft are advised.

items.front: What does the FFM remote status alarm actually indicate?
items.back: An inoperative or out-of-tolerance localizer signal (not glideslope). The course may have shifted due to equipment malfunction or vehicle/aircraft encroachment into the critical area.

items.front: When must the FFM remote sensing unit be operational?
items.back: When weather is below CAT II ILS minimums.

items.front: What are the three different controller responses to an FFM alarm, based on the aircraft's position on final?
items.back: 1) Outside MM/1/2 mile final: check for visible critical-area encroachment; if alarm hasn't cleared by MM/1/2 mile, issue advisory. 2) Between MM and IM (or CAT II MAP): immediately issue advisory. 3) Past IM/CAT II MAP: no action required.
